Since I posted about AI and Radiology earlier this week, 3 things every Auditor and risk professional should know.

𝟭. 𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝗯𝗲𝗮𝗿𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗖𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗜 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘀 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴?
Not the hospital CEO, not the chip company and not the software vendor but the clinician who acted on the output. AI medical liability falls under state tort law with little precedent. Courts expect human oversight, a physician who followed a faulty AI recommendation without independent judgment can be held liable.

The governance irony: the radiologist IS the loop, legally, professionally, clinically. Remove them and you remove the only party absorbing liability with no framework to replace them. That's not cost saving. That's risk transfer with nowhere for the risk to go.

𝟮. 𝗘𝘅𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗱𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘁𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗴𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗳𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘂𝗿𝗲, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘆 𝗶𝘀𝘀𝘂𝗲.
When CEO of NYC Health + Hospitals and CEO of NVIDIA publicly discussed replacing radiologists, radiologists learned from news coverage. This is not a communication failure but a governance failure.

Domain experts aren't stakeholders to brief after the fact. They're the only people who:
→ Know where edge cases live that benchmarks don't test
→ Understand what failure costs patients, not just balance sheets
→ Identify automation bias before it's embedded in workflow
→ Define what the standard of care actually is

The audit question isn't "were experts consulted? It's "at what point, with what authority to change the outcome?" A radiologist briefed after contract signing is communication. One defining validation criteria before procurement is governance.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆.
Over 700 FDA-cleared AI tools are deployed in imaging, yet 4,333 radiology job listings from Q1 2026 signaled salary, schedule, and partnership. Not AI competency.

→ 77% of organizations are building AI governance programs
→ Only 1.5% are satisfied with their governance headcount
→ AI skills are nearly absent from hiring requirements

Revealed preference: hiring requirements tell you more than CEO panels. AI is being bolted onto existing workflows without changing the human infrastructure around it. If your job descriptions haven't changed, you don't have an AI strategy. You have an AI hope.

4 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗔𝗜 𝗮𝘂𝗱𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗯𝗲 𝗮𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴:
→ Who validated this?
→ Who bears liability?
→ Who was not in the room?
→ 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗷𝗼𝗯 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗸𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝗱𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀?

Radiology is not a special case. Narratives outrunning reality, experts excluded, liability unresolved, workforce readiness ignored, this pattern spans healthcare, finance, legal, and hiring AI.

Your AI transformation isn't stalling because of the technology.
It's stalling because your tech org was designed for 2015.

McKinsey's latest research: Two-thirds of top-performing companies have their technology leaders deeply involved in enterprise strategy. 

Most FSI CIOs are still handed an execution mandate on a strategy they didn't shape.

Three moves separate companies pulling ahead from those falling behind:

- The junior developer headcount model is finished. Agentic AI is absorbing that work. Demand is shifting to senior engineers and architects who orchestrate human-agent teams at scale. Companies hiring for volume are inflating costs without increasing output.

-Vendor contracts built on seat counts are dead weight. One bank introduced AI-driven automation targets across every strategic vendor — a 10% productivity uplift followed. Most FSI contracts still reward effort, not outcomes.

-Reskilling is a CEO-CIO-CHRO mandate, not an L&D programme. The companies winning have mapped role-level skill proficiency, found untapped expertise, and built hybrid human-agent teams with real change management behind them.

McKinsey says the right formula can triple your technology ROI. The wrong one locks you in permanently.

The quiet erosion of India's engineering advantage
I'm sure most of us have noticed things changing over the past year. Your peers on the management ladder in IT companies have suddenly started talking about job security, realising that their work of managing teams of coders is becoming irrelevant. The neighbour's kid, graduating from a reputed college with a fancy AI/ML degree, is still looking for a job. And newspapers scream with colleges competing to assure job guarantees. The ad space once occupied by Allen's and Akash's of the world has now been taken over by Galgotias and Lovely universities.
We're all witnessing a structural shift. For thirty years, we ran one of the most successful economic playbooks in history:                                                     Train engineers at scale → Export services globally → Create millions of jobs → Make "engineer" the default path to middle-class security.                          It worked spectacularly. It built cities, funded dreams, sent kids abroad, and convinced an entire generation of parents that if their child becomes an engineer, they'll be safe.
That safety net is being rewoven in real-time. AI is rewriting what engineering work means. When code can be generated in seconds, the valuable question shifts from "who writes this?" to "who decides what to build, how systems connect, and whether solutions actually work in the real world?" That's a fundamentally different skill set. And we're not training for it at scale.
Every country that has navigated a structural economic transition has paid a price. The UK moving from manufacturing to services. The US shifting from industrial work to tech leadership. Japan optimizing itself into stagnation. None were sudden collapses — they were slow rebalancings that took years to become obvious.                                                                                    India's version may be faster as AI is moving at breakneck speed: Entry-level hiring quietly slowing down, widening performance gap between top 5% and average graduates, educational institutions still optimizing for yesterday's market, families confused why the old formula stopped working.
In India, "Engineer" isn't just a profession; it's a social contract. A certainty. When that certainty cracks, the anxiety ripples through households and classrooms long before it shows up in economic data.
The opportunity has relocated. The next decade will reward people who can operate where problems aren't predefined, software meets physical reality, and judgment matters more than execution speed. Context, systems thinking, and integration become the real differentiators.
We'll face prolonged unemployment and economic pain unless we fundamentally reposition ourselves. AI has already decided that change is coming. We can either transform into builders and creators now, or watch the market make that decision for us later.

Last night I joined the D2 collective for a panel on hiring and talent in the age of AI - co-sponsored by Workable

We spent a lot of time reflecting on how AI will continue to get better at the information-heavy, low context work, and how people leaders need to stay connected to the human outcomes we're ultimately driving towards.

My favorite quote right now in this AI era: "Humans are not thinking machines that feel. We are feeling machines that think." - Antonio Damasio, Neuroscientist/Professor 

Case in point. Someone approached me after the panel to share they'd been choosing between two ATS vendors - comparable products, comparable pricing. One rep spoke generously about the competition. The other didn't. The contract went to the generous one.

This is data that reveals how the company would show up as a partner - when things got complicated, when something broke, when it was time to renew. The feeling that was produced was real information and the ultimate reason for buying the product. 

Similarly, your company work environment is a product you want someone to "buy into". Your hiring process, your culture, your strategic AI technology deployments - all of it creates a feeling that informs action. And until we're all replaced by bots, this is how 95% of all decisions get made by the humans still making them. 

Thank you to Erica Isotta Surace and Nastie Schoenmakers for organizing and bringing this conversation to life. And special thanks to my fellow panelists, I learned a lot from the various industry perspectives! Katie Concannon, Chloe Plum, SHRM-CP and Daniel Minjock

Upwork just made a big move, Uma is now a selectable option right from the start.

When you create a new job post on Upwork, you'll now see Uma as a job type option at the very beginning. AI-powered hiring isn't buried in the background anymore, it's front and center.

And here's the real shift — Upwork is now a PPC + AI game.
Freelancers who aren't adapting to this new reality are already falling behind. Visibility on Upwork is no longer just about having a great profile. It's about understanding how Pay-Per-Click (Connects) and AI-driven selection work together. Uma decides who gets shortlisted, and PPC determines who gets seen. If you're not optimizing for both, you're invisible.

But here's the elephant in the room — why aren't clients hiring?

It's the fees. Plain and simple.
Before, client fees were around 5%. With VAT, card processing fees, and bank charges, clients were paying roughly 8-10% per job to Upwork. That was already painful but some manageable.

Now? Client fees have doubled to 10% for business plan. Add VAT, credit card fees, state taxes, transaction charges, and contract initiation fees on top of that and clients are now paying nearly 15-18% of every job to Upwork.

Let that sink in. For every $1,000 project, almost $180 goes to platform fees just client alone.

These fees are becoming unbearable for clients. And the result?
Upwork quietly shifted their giant, high-value clients to their new enterprise platform, Lifted. What's left on the main Upwork marketplace? Budget clients, low-quality jobs, and a race to the bottom.

The premium clients are gone. The big contracts are gone. And freelancers are left fighting over scraps with inflated Connect costs while clients struggle to justify the fees.

Meanwhile, Uma is doing this behind the scenes:
It scans 2.2 million+ active freelancer profiles, reasons through job requirements using agentic AI, sends invites to the top 20 matches, and ranks all proposals to surface a Top 5 shortlist.

The ranking is based on skill relevance, work history, Job Success Score, and alignment with must-have requirements, all powered by OpenAI's large language models, fine-tuned with Upwork's proprietary data.

What Uma can do: 
→ Draft job posts from just a few keywords 
→ Explain why a freelancer is a strong match 
→ Evaluate thousands of profiles in seconds 
→ Deliver a ready-to-review shortlist automatically

There's also a ChatGPT × Upwork integration, brainstorm in ChatGPT and hand it off to Uma for talent matching.

On privacy: Your data is not used to train OpenAI's public models.

Let's be real:
Upwork is no longer a freelance marketplace. It's an AI-first, PPC-driven, fee-heavy platform that's pricing out good clients and pushing premium business to Lifted.

The old Upwork is dead.

The question is, are you going to keep playing a rigged game, or are you going to adapt your strategy?

Nobody told data engineers the most important truth about Databricks and Palantir — they were never actually competing.

I've spent years working across enterprise stacks building ML pipelines, lakehouses, and real-time streaming architectures.

And I kept running into the same painful pattern👇
You build a beautiful ML model in your Lakehouse. 
PySpark optimized. Unity Catalog governed. Delta Lake powered. It's perfect on paper.

Then it sits. Unused. Because the supply chain manager, the compliance officer, the field technician — they can't interact with it.

That's the last-mile AI problem nobody talks about.
Databricks builds the AI. Palantir deploys AI into the hands of people who make real decisions.

Here's the difference that actually matters:
🔵 Databricks = Engineering-first → Lakehouse architecture. Delta Lake. Spark at scale. → Your data engineers and data scientists LOVE it. → Open. Flexible. Build whatever you want.
🟡 Palantir Foundry = Decision-first → Ontology layer that maps data to real business objects. → A maintenance tech, a risk officer, an ops lead — they can USE it. → Governance and auditability baked in by design.

The philosophical gap: 
➡ Databricks output = models, pipelines, data products 
➡ Palantir output = workflows, applications, operational AI decisions

And now? They're officially partners.
Palantir's Ontology + Databricks' Unity Catalog + Delta Sharing = enterprises can finally close the loop between raw data → trusted AI → real-world action.

In my experience, the teams that win aren't using one or the other. They're using Databricks to engineer the foundation — and Palantir to operationalize the outcomes.

The orgs still debating "which one" are asking the wrong question entirely.

💬 Where does your org struggle most — building the AI pipeline, or actually getting business users to act on it?

Stellantis just handed Microsoft a five year contract to migrate their entire global infrastructure to Azure and build 100+ AI initiatives together.
The headline says this is about predictive maintenance, digital cabins, and cybersecurity.
I read it differently.
This is a data engineering problem wearing a car company's clothes.
Think about what actually has to happen before a single AI model predicts a manufacturing defect or a digital cabin feature personalizes a driver experience. Someone has to ingest telemetry data from hundreds of thousands of vehicles. Someone has to normalize sensor data from factories across three continents that were never designed to talk to each other. Someone has to build the pipelines that take raw operational data from legacy on-premises systems and land it clean and governed in Azure Data Lake. Someone has to make sure the feature store feeding those 100 AI initiatives is accurate, fresh, and trusted.
That someone is a data engineer.
And here is the thing TSMC quietly confirmed this same week. They are spending between $52 and $56 billion on AI infrastructure this year because the demand for AI compute is not slowing down. Every dollar of that spend eventually needs clean data flowing through it to produce anything useful.
I have been building Azure data pipelines at Elevance Health for two years. Healthcare and automotive are very different domains. But the moment a Fortune 500 company decides to bet its entire infrastructure on Azure and AI, the data engineering work that follows looks remarkably similar. Multi-source ingestion from systems that were never meant to integrate. Medallion Architecture to move raw data into something trusted. Data quality frameworks to catch the problems before they reach the model. Governance to satisfy the auditors.
What the Stellantis deal tells me is not that Microsoft won a contract.
It tells me that every large enterprise watching this deal is quietly asking their leadership the same question right now. Are we ready to do the same? And the honest answer for most of them is that they are not ready. Not because they lack the cloud budget. Because they lack the data platform foundation underneath it.
That foundation is what data engineers build.
The next wave of Azure data engineering hiring is not coming. It is already here.
Are you seeing more Azure data platform conversations starting at your organization?

“I'm pretty bullish on OpenAI in enterprise”

“ if your agents are only 60% as good, you're in a slow death spiral.”

The BIGGEST news in tech this week:

- Anthropic Unveils Mythos
- SpaceX's Financials Leaked: Is it Worth $2TRN
- Meta Debuts Muse Spark: Are They Back in the AI Race
- Jason's Critique of Dario Amodei & How OpenAI Could Win the Enterprise Game

The only podcast you have to listen to every week.

No politics. Just tech.

My 6 lessons with Jason Lemkin and Rory O'Driscoll

1. The Incumbent’s Big Problem and Why the Saas-Pocalypse Might Be Fair
Incumbents face a "doom loop" by building AI products only 60% as good as specialized competitors. While internal teams are proud, the market won't pay for mediocre "check the box" features. To avoid a slow death spiral, companies must build agents good enough to charge for independently, as a 60% solution must ultimately be free.

2. Why the Whole Moat Discussion Is Bullshit
Traditional moats often act as prisons for customers rather than magnets for new ones. Long-term contracts keep users trapped but fail to generate "agentic revenue" or market excitement. True growth requires moving beyond defensive barriers to lure customers through innovation, as "prisoners" do not drive meaningful expansion.

3. Silicon Valley Thinks They’re Reinventing the World and They’re Not
Silicon Valley’s "overwrought intensity" and grandiosity serve as a powerful rallying cry for talent. Even if visions of massive unemployment are "airy fairy bullshit," this energy keeps the innovation machine churning and provides mission clarity. Investors should focus on whether this idealism motivates teams enough to build trillion-dollar market caps.

4. The Quantum Leap of Agentic Cybersecurity
Anthropic’s Mythos model marks a quantum leap by autonomously finding code vulnerabilities. This agentic approach is the difference between a rifle and a machine gun, spewing "bullets" across large codebases. We are entering a transition phase where security may worsen as every site becomes instantly scannable and breachable by bad actors.

5. The Trillion-Dollar Ad Race and the Shift to Enterprise
OpenAI’s goal of $53 billion in ad revenue by 2029 is a logical move for a consumer product. However, ads alone won’t sustain massive burn rates; enterprise intelligence likely represents two-thirds of AI's total value. While consumers want entertainment, corporations are paying for "thinking" and cognition.

6. Why Efficiency is Now a Strategic Choice, Not Just a Metric
High revenue per head is now a deliberate choice enabled by AI agents. The new team evaluation test is no longer just about re-hiring, but whether you would rather replace a person with a trained agent. Founders who fail to improve efficiency risk becoming stable but irrelevant "value plays" like IBM.

AI is not replacing IT professionals in Canada. It's making the wrong IT hire far more expensive.

There's a narrative circulating that AI is eliminating IT jobs. The data tells a more nuanced story.
 
Statistics Canada reports that 57.4% of Canadian jobs are now highly exposed to AI. But of those, 51% are AI-augmenting roles, where AI enhances what the professional does, rather than replacing them.
 
What's actually happening in the Canadian IT market:
 
→ Demand for AI-adjacent skills (ML engineering, data architecture, AI governance) is surging
→ Organizations that deployed AI without the right technical talent to manage it are now scrambling to backfill
→ Junior roles in data entry and administration are shrinking but senior technical and delivery roles are growing
→ 67% of tech leaders say critical thinking and adaptability are now the top skills they're hiring for, not just technical certifications
 
The organizations getting caught flat-footed are those who assumed AI would reduce their need for IT contract talent. Many are now returning to the market 6–12 months later, needing more specialized professionals than before at higher rates, in a tighter market.
 
The IT professionals who thrive in 2026 are not those who avoid AI. They're the ones who understand how to work alongside it.
 
And the organizations that thrive are the ones who hire those professionals before their competitors do.
 
BQ International places IT contract professionals at every stage of the delivery lifecycle, from discovery to deployment. If AI transformation is on your roadmap, the right talent foundation matters.

AI is not (yet) the primary driver of job loss.

◉ 60–70% of knowledge work tasks can be automated or augmented

Meanwhile:

◉ Job openings are down significantly
◉ Hiring is slowing but there's an increase in demand for contract work
◉ Organizations are redesigning work, not just reducing it

The shift is already happening, just not in the way most headlines suggest.

Certification vs Experience: What U.S. Tech Recruiters Actually Prefer in 2026
After placing hundreds of tech professionals this year, here's the straight talk from the trenches:
✅ Experience wins for proven delivery, Real-world projects, problem-solving under pressure, and the ability to ship code/features that drive business results still matter most. Recruiters and hiring managers repeatedly say: "Certs get you in the door, but experience gets you the offer."
✅ Certifications are powerful accelerators, Especially in cloud (AWS, Azure), cybersecurity, and AI. They:

Help career switchers and juniors stand out
Signal continuous learning and up-to-date skills
Are often required or heavily preferred in regulated or fast-evolving fields

The 2026 reality? Hybrid profiles dominate. Candidates who combine solid hands-on experience with relevant, vendor-recognized certifications (AWS Solutions Architect, CompTIA Security+, Azure, etc.) get interviews faster and command stronger rates.
Skills-based hiring is growing, but "skills + proof of application" beats theory every time.
At WebMobileZ, we match talent that brings both ,because that's what our clients demand for contract, contract-to-hire, and direct-hire roles.

Unpopular opinion: I told 4 clients this quarter to STOP their AI projects.

They were all paying me to build AI solutions. I told them not to.
Here's why.

Client 1: Wanted AI to write performance reviews. The problem? Performance reviews require empathy, context about someone's personal growth, and political awareness. AI can draft templates. It can't replace a manager who actually knows their team.

Client 2: Wanted to automate their entire hiring pipeline with AI. Red flag. Their pipeline was broken BEFORE AI. Bad job descriptions. No structured interviews. Inconsistent criteria. AI would've just scaled their dysfunction faster.

Client 3: Wanted AI to fully replace their legal review process. Contract review with zero human oversight. Too high-stakes. One missed clause in a vendor agreement could cost more than the entire AI budget. AI can assist legal — it can't BE legal.

Client 4: Wanted AI to replace their 3-person content team. Their content team was their competitive advantage. Unique voice. Deep industry knowledge. Replacing them with AI would've turned their brand into generic noise.

4 different companies. Same mistake.

They were so excited about AI that they forgot to ask: "Is this actually a problem that needs AI?"

Sometimes the answer is a better process. Sometimes it's a $50 spreadsheet. Sometimes it's just hiring one more person.

AI is a tool. And the best thing about a good tool is knowing when to leave it in the toolbox.

What's an AI use case you've seen that definitely should NOT have been an AI use case?

What the **? 6.5 out of 10. That's what AI scored my CV.

It didn't know I was laid off twice.

It didn't know the 1st time. I sat in my home for 3 weeks, wondering what I did wrong. The 2nd time, I watched an entire team get dissolved on a Tuesday morning Google Meet call.

It just saw the pattern break. 2 contracts back to back. Short stints. No "promotion" in the title.
& it did what AI does. It penalized it.

🔴 AI scored me 6.5 
🟢 AI with a human lens scored me 8.2

Same resume. Same system. The only difference? One was taught to ask "why" before it penalizes.

Here's what default AI doesn't do when it sees a gap on your resume:
It doesn't think "oh shit, 2022 to 2026 was a bloodbath."
It doesn't remember that entire teams got wiped out overnight. That SaaS companies went from hiring 200 people a quarter to freezing headcount in a single board meeting.
It doesn't know that the person with 3 contract roles in 4 years wasn't job-hopping. They were surviving.

👀 AI sees a lateral title move. It doesn't see the person who took a step sideways because the company that promoted them no longer exists.
👀 AI sees "no certifications." It doesn't see someone who spent 300+ hours teaching themselves because no employer was paying for their development anymore.
👀 AI sees an Indian phone number with no location listed. It doesn't see someone who supported hiring across 38+ countries from a home office because that's what remote work actually looks like.
👀 AI sees a regional university. It doesn't see 240+ hours of mentoring across 30+ countries, ranked Top 1% on ADPList. Because that's not a credential it can parse.

The gap between 6.5 & 8.2 isn't a math problem.

It's an empathy problem.
Default AI reads your CV like a spreadsheet. AI with a human lens reads it like a story.
& every story has chapters that only make sense if you know what was happening in the world when they were written.

I know this because I build AI evaluation & screening systems for a living. 200+ of them. Scaled across global hiring.
& the single hardest thing to engineer into any of them?

Context. Empathy. The ability to look at a pattern break & ask "why" before asking "how much do I penalize this."

That's the difference between AI that screens & AI that understands.
Your CV is not a 6.5. The AI just hasn't been taught your story yet.

What's the chapter on your CV that AI would never understand? 👇

The UK's Financial Reporting Council just published the first regulatory guidance in the world on AI use in auditing.

The core principle: "It is people who are accountable for audit quality." Not the AI, not the vendor, not the firm's technology stack. The individual auditor.

The FRC is explicit. Audit partners bear full accountability for AI-assisted work, including failures caused by hallucinations, data distortions, or what the guidance calls "misuse" of AI outputs. If the AI gets it wrong and the auditor signs off, that's not a technology failure. That's an audit failure. Same consequences, same liability, same career risk.

This landed the same week EY rolled out agentic AI across 130,000 assurance professionals globally and KPMG US started piloting AI orchestration agents on its Clara platform. The Big Four are deploying AI into audit at production scale. And the regulator just told every partner at those firms: if it breaks, it's on you personally.

Think about what that does to the adoption calculus. You're an audit partner. The firm hands you an AI tool that processes journal entries, summarizes board minutes, reviews contracts for revenue recognition. It's faster. It catches things you'd miss. But if it hallucinates a data point and you don't catch it, your name is on the opinion letter.

Tom Hood, has spent years advocating for technology adoption across the accounting profession through his work at the AICPA. But this guidance changes the evaluation framework for every firm leader in that conversation. It's no longer "does this tool improve efficiency?" It's "would I stake my professional license on this tool's output?"

Glenn Hopper has been building frameworks for how finance leaders should evaluate AI tools, including through his courses at Duke and his writing on AI in corporate finance. The FRC just added a variable most of those frameworks don't account for: personal regulatory liability. That's a fundamentally different adoption curve than what the market is pricing in.

And it might explain why the firms making the biggest AI investments are also the ones hiring the most aggressively. They're not replacing judgment. They're adding a layer of humans whose entire job is making sure the AI didn't get it wrong.

The FRC just answered the accountability question the industry has been debating for two years. The answer is: it's always been the human.

The BPO model isn’t being disrupted. It’s dying in plain sight.

Just look at some of the latest stocks of outsourcing CX monsters since the birth of GPT (2022)  :

📉 Teleperformance: Stock down 85%+ from peak. 
📉 Concentrix: Stock down 82% from peak.
📉 TTEC: Stock down 98% from peak.  

While the "old world" evaporates, the new world is scaling at the speed of light: 

- Sierra, one of our competitors ( that we love beating at boost.ai) : Hit $100M ARR in just 7 quarters. Now valued at $10B.  

- Decagon hit a 4.5B valuation from 650M in 18 month 
- Wonderful.AI 


This is the largest transfer of value I’ve seen in my career. And here’s why most people are still missing the point:
AI is not "improving" customer service. It is replacing the operating model behind it.

I’ve spent the last 2 years architecting multi-agent workflows. When you’re inside the builds, the shift isn’t theoretical. It’s mechanical.
• AI doesn’t have a "hiring ramp."
• AI doesn’t need a middle manager to monitor its "Average Handle Time."
• AI doesn’t care about time zones, attrition, or scaling plans.

It just executes—instantly, at near-zero marginal cost.

When companies say, "We’re adding AI to our support operations," they are usually just putting a motor on a horse. 

It’s still a horse. 

It’s still a labor-heavy system with a tech "patch."


The Real Shift:
Service is moving from People + Process → Software + Orchestration.

Humans aren’t going away, but their role is compressing into two zones:

1.	High-Trust: Interactions where empathy is the product.
2.	Edge Cases: Complexity that software hasn't mapped yet.

Everything else? It's being absorbed by agentic workflows.


The uncomfortable truth: Most enterprise BPO contracts haven't felt the "floor" yet. But they will at renewal. That’s when the CFO realizes they don't need 1,000 seats; they need a better architect.

I’ve seen how fast a CEO goes from "Is AI safe?" to "How fast can we roll this out everywhere?"

This isn’t just a tech trend. It’s a full reset of how service businesses are built and valued.

The Question:
Are AI agents just an "optimization" tool for you... or are we watching the final collapse of an obosolete industry model?
Curious to hear from those in the trenches.

18 months ago, a client based in Singapore came to us with a real problem.

Strong brand. Great positioning. But pipeline? Unpredictable.

Brokers were eating into margins.

The internal team were closers, not prospectors.

And their own outbound attempts? 2 face-to-face meetings per quarter.

They didn't need another hire.

They needed a system.

So we built one.

Here's what we ran:

→ Built a list of decision-makers with private office buying intent using Clay and AI Ark. Not just "companies in Singapore."

→ Stacked buying signals with Trigify.io and RB2B. Hiring ops roles, opening a SG presence, just raised funding. We only reached out when timing made sense.

→ Enriched every contact through a waterfall across Icypeas, LeadMagic, and BetterContact. Verified emails and phone numbers before a single message went out.

→ Multi-channel sequences across cold email (Smartlead + Zapmail) and LinkedIn (HeyReach) . Premium positioning from the first message. No discount language.

→ Pre-qualified every lead before it hit the sales team. Tours are expensive. Only serious buyers got in the door.

The results?

Drop-off rate went from ~50% to ~30%.

Meetings went from 2 per quarter to 4 per month.

And roughly 9 months in, a single deal closed at $250K contract value.

That one deal alone covered the full collaboration.

Zero extra headcount. Zero broker fees.

Just a system running in the background.

Outbound works when you build it properly.

But it takes time, testing, and a lot of iteration 😅

Role: AI/ML Solution Architect
Location: Remote

We are seeking an experienced AI/ML Solution Architect with strong expertise in Microsoft Copilot, Azure services, Python, and PySpark. Support cloud modernization initiatives and architecture reviews.

8+ years of experience in software engineering / data engineering / solution architecture roles.

Hands-on expertise in Python and PySpark for data processing and model development.
Experience with Copilot / AI copilots / Generative AI integration and solutioning.

Ability to lead technical discussions, solution design sessions, and architecture reviews.

Knowledge of Databricks, delta lakes, and large-scale distributed systems.
Microsoft Azure certifications (Architect, Data Engineer, AI Engineer).
